does: This paper empirically demonstrates that state-of-the-art image generation and reward models exhibit systematic bias toward conventionally aesthetic outputs, failing to produce anti-aesthetic imagery even when explicitly requested. The authors construct a wide-spectrum aesthetics dataset and evaluate current models through direct prompting, image-to-image editing, and comparison against real abstract artworks. Their core finding is that reward models consistently penalize anti-aesthetic outputs that perfectly match user instructions, while generation models default to beautified versions of prompts that request ugly, distorted, or otherwise unconventional visual styles. The paper positions this as a critical failure mode where developer-centered aesthetic preferences override explicit user intent and constrain the space of possible artistic expression.
connects: This work builds on growing critique of value alignment in machine learning, particularly recent discussions of how RLHF and reward modeling can embed narrow preferences that conflict with user autonomy and pluralism. It extends concerns about bias in large generative models by focusing specifically on aesthetic dimensions—a domain where prior work on image generation benchmarks and model evaluation has often treated beauty as a unidimensional good rather than a contested cultural value. The paper connects to scholarship on digital aesthetics and critical AI art that questions assumptions embedded in training data and learning objectives, while also drawing on work examining how reward models learn proxy preferences that diverge from stated user goals.
enables: This position paper opens directions for designing generation and reward models that respect aesthetic pluralism and user intent across the full spectrum of visual expression. It motivates research into conditional aesthetics training, where models learn to align outputs to user-specified aesthetic goals rather than a fixed developer preference. Future work could explore how to construct reward models that recognize anti-aesthetic imagery as valid when intentional, develop evaluation frameworks that measure fidelity to intent rather than conventional beauty, and investigate how alignment techniques can preserve artistic expression for purposes including satire, critique, and experimental art. The released datasets and code provide infrastructure for ongoing investigation of aesthetic bias in generative AI systems.
